questions.q: Fixed-window rate limit of 100 req/min. A client sends 100 at 12:00:59 and 100 at 12:01:01. What went wrong?
choices: 200 requests in 2 seconds (2× the intended rate)
questions.explanation: Fixed-window's 'boundary burst': window resets at minute boundary. Client fits a full 100 at the end of one window and 100 at the start of the next, landing 200 in seconds. Sliding-window smooths this.

questions.q: Token bucket allows burst + sustained rate. Which knob controls BURST tolerance?
questions.explanation: Capacity = max tokens the bucket can hold = max burst size. Refill rate = sustained allowed rate. A 100-token bucket at 10 refills/sec: sustained 10/sec, burst up to 100.

questions.q: Leaky bucket vs token bucket: which outputs a STRICTLY smooth stream regardless of input burstiness?
questions.explanation: Leaky bucket drains at constant rate — output is smooth even if input is bursty (excess is dropped/queued). Token bucket lets bursts through if tokens are available.

questions.q: Distributed rate limiter across 100 app servers. What's the simplest correct design?
choices: Centralized Redis INCR + TTL per key
questions.explanation: Per-server counters are trivially bypassed (rotate through IPs). Redis INCR with TTL gives atomic centralized counting. Some trade exactness for perf via local batches + periodic sync.

questions.q: 429 Too Many Requests. What HTTP header tells the client when to retry?
questions.explanation: Retry-After (standard HTTP) gives seconds or date. X-RateLimit-* (optional) expose the whole budget. Always include Retry-After — well-behaved clients will obey.

questions.q: On Redis outage, a rate limiter should typically fail…
questions.explanation: Fail-open: better to temporarily over-allow than block every request. Exception: auth-critical paths (e.g., anti-brute-force on login) should fail-closed. Choose per endpoint.

questions.q: Your API gateway rate-limits at 1000 req/sec per API key. A client sends 999 requests at T=0.999 and 999 at T=1.001. The fixed-window counter sees 999 in each window. But the actual rate over that 2ms span is ~999K req/sec. What algorithm fixes this?
choices: Sliding window log or sliding window counter
questions.explanation: Sliding window log tracks each request timestamp and counts within a rolling window. Sliding window counter approximates this by weighting the previous window's count. Both prevent the boundary-burst problem where two full allowances land in a tiny real-time window.

questions.q: Your distributed rate limiter uses Redis INCR with a 60-second TTL. During a Redis master failover (5 seconds), the new replica has stale counters that are 3 seconds behind. What's the impact?
choices: Some users get a 'free' burst because their counters reset to an older (lower) value
No impact — Redis Sentinel handles it
questions.explanation: Redis async replication means the new master may have counters from 3 seconds ago. Users who were near their limit get headroom back. For most APIs this is acceptable. For critical paths (payment, auth), consider a local fallback counter during failover.

questions.q: You have rate limiting at both the API gateway (global: 10K/min per tenant) and individual microservices (per-service: 1K/min per tenant). A tenant's requests pass the gateway but get 429s from Service B. What's happening?
choices: The tenant's traffic pattern hits Service B disproportionately — within global budget but exceeding Service B's local limit
questions.explanation: Multi-tier rate limiting creates this conflict. A tenant using 9K/min on Service A and 1.5K/min on Service B is within the 10K global limit but over Service B's 1K local limit. Solutions: communicate remaining budget across tiers, or set local limits as fractions of the global budget with headroom.

questions.q: A mobile app retries immediately on receiving a 429 response, ignoring the Retry-After header. Under load, this creates a feedback loop. What pattern emerges?
choices: Retry storm: rejected requests instantly retry, doubling the load on the rate limiter and making congestion worse
questions.explanation: Aggressive retries on 429 amplify load: each rejected request becomes two requests. This positive feedback loop can overwhelm the rate limiter itself. Clients must respect Retry-After, use exponential backoff with jitter, and cap max retries. Server-side, consider returning 503 with backoff hints during extreme overload.

questions.q: Your rate limiter uses a token bucket per user with 100 tokens refilling at 10/sec. A legitimate user needs to upload 200 small files in a batch job. They hit the rate limit after 100 files. What's the best accommodation without removing the rate limit?
choices: Remove the rate limit for this user
Provide a separate bulk/batch API endpoint with a higher per-request allowance or batch grouping, keeping the per-item rate limit for the standard API
Increase all users' bucket size to 200
Tell the user to add delays
questions.explanation: A dedicated batch endpoint that accepts multiple items per request lets the user accomplish the same work within rate limits. The standard API keeps its protective limits for normal traffic. This is how S3 (multi-object delete), Gmail (batch API), and Stripe (bulk creates) handle it.
